questions.question: 1. What is a decision tree mainly used for in machine learning?
options: For clustering unlabelled samples into groups
For compressing feature matrices before training
For supervised classification and regression tasks
For visualizing the layers of a neural network
questions.explanation: A decision tree is a supervised learning model that can handle classification and regression. It predicts by following feature-based decisions from the root to a leaf.

mini_glossary.Decision tree: A model that predicts by following a tree of decisions.
mini_glossary.Classification: Prediction of a discrete class label.

questions.frage: 1. What is a decision tree mainly used for in machine learning?
optionen: For clustering unlabelled samples into groups
For compressing feature matrices before training
For supervised classification and regression tasks
For visualizing the layers of a neural network
questions.erklaerung: A decision tree is a supervised learning model that can handle classification and regression. It predicts by following feature-based decisions from the root to a leaf.

questions.question: 2. In a decision tree, what is a leaf?
options: A terminal node that stores the final prediction
The first node where every prediction starts
A feature threshold selected before training
A scaling parameter applied to all inputs
questions.explanation: A leaf is a terminal node with no further children. Once an instance reaches a leaf, the tree returns the prediction stored there.

mini_glossary.Leaf: A final node that produces a prediction.
mini_glossary.Node: A point in the tree where a test or prediction is stored.
mini_glossary.Child node: A node reached after a split.

questions.question: 3. When is the Gini impurity of a node equal to zero?
options: When the node contains equal class proportions
When the tree has reached its maximum depth
When all features have been standardized
When all samples in the node belong to one class
questions.explanation: A node is pure when all samples in it belong to one class. In that case, the Gini impurity is zero because there is no class mixture.

mini_glossary.Gini impurity: A measure of class mixture inside a node.
mini_glossary.Pure node: A node containing samples from only one class.

questions.frage: 3. When is the Gini impurity of a node equal to zero?
optionen: When the node contains equal class proportions
When the tree has reached its maximum depth
When all features have been standardized
When all samples in the node belong to one class
questions.erklaerung: A node is pure when all samples in it belong to one class. In that case, the Gini impurity is zero because there is no class mixture.

questions.question: 4. Which hyperparameter directly limits the maximum depth of a decision tree in Scikit-Learn?
options: `min_samples_leaf`
`max_depth`
`criterion`
`random_state`
questions.explanation: `max_depth` limits how many levels the tree may grow. Lowering it is a common regularization strategy for decision trees.

mini_glossary.max_depth: The maximum allowed depth of a tree.
mini_glossary.Regularization: A restriction that reduces model flexibility.
mini_glossary.Overfitting: Fitting training noise instead of general patterns.

questions.frage: 4. Which hyperparameter directly limits the maximum depth of a decision tree in Scikit-Learn?
optionen: `min_samples_leaf`
`max_depth`
`criterion`
`random_state`
questions.erklaerung: `max_depth` limits how many levels the tree may grow. Lowering it is a common regularization strategy for decision trees.

questions.question: 5. In a regression tree, what does a leaf typically predict?
options: The most frequent class in the training set
The feature with the largest split gain
The mean target value of the samples in that leaf
The probability assigned to the largest class
questions.explanation: A regression tree predicts numerical values rather than class labels. A leaf usually stores the mean target value of the training samples that reach that leaf.

mini_glossary.Regression tree: A decision tree that predicts numerical values.
mini_glossary.Target value: The value the model is trained to predict.

questions.frage: 5. In a regression tree, what does a leaf typically predict?
optionen: The most frequent class in the training set
The feature with the largest split gain
The mean target value of the samples in that leaf
The probability assigned to the largest class
questions.erklaerung: A regression tree predicts numerical values rather than class labels. A leaf usually stores the mean target value of the training samples that reach that leaf.

questions.question: 6. A trained tree first tests petal length and then, only for some cases, tests petal width. What does this illustrate?
options: The model averages all possible paths.
The prediction follows one path from root to leaf.
Every feature is tested before a prediction is returned.
Two features cannot appear in the same tree.
questions.explanation: For one input instance, a decision tree follows exactly one route through the tree. It starts at the root and applies only the tests needed along that route.

mini_glossary.Root: The first node of a tree.
mini_glossary.Path: The sequence of nodes visited during prediction.
mini_glossary.Split: A test that sends samples to child nodes.

extended_explanation.title: Following a prediction path
extended_explanation.content: The important idea is that a decision tree does not combine all branches for one prediction. It applies a sequence of local decisions until it reaches a final prediction.
steps: Start at the root node.
Evaluate the feature test stored in the node.
Move to exactly one child node.
Repeat until a leaf is reached.

questions.frage: 6. A trained tree first tests petal length and then, only for some cases, tests petal width. What does this illustrate?
optionen: The model averages all possible paths.
The prediction follows one path from root to leaf.
Every feature is tested before a prediction is returned.
Two features cannot appear in the same tree.
questions.erklaerung: For one input instance, a decision tree follows exactly one route through the tree. It starts at the root and applies only the tests needed along that route.

questions.question: 7. A classification node has `samples = 100` and `value = [0, 49, 51]`. What does this mean?
options: The node has 100 child nodes and three possible labels.
The node used 100 features during split selection.
The node contains 100 samples split across three classes.
The node stores three thresholds and 100 prediction errors.
questions.explanation: `samples` is the number of training instances that reach the node. `value` stores the class counts for those instances.

mini_glossary.samples: The number of training instances reaching a node.
mini_glossary.value: The class counts stored in a classification node.
mini_glossary.Class count: The number of samples belonging to each class.

extended_explanation.title: Reading node statistics
extended_explanation.content: Node statistics explain why a decision tree predicts a class. They show how the training data were distributed at the point where the prediction is made.
steps: Read `samples` as the total number of samples in the node.
Read each entry of `value` as one class count.
Check that the entries of `value` sum to `samples`.
Use the largest class count to identify the predicted class.

questions.frage: 7. A classification node has `samples = 100` and `value = [0, 49, 51]`. What does this mean?
optionen: The node has 100 child nodes and three possible labels.
The node used 100 features during split selection.
The node contains 100 samples split across three classes.
The node stores three thresholds and 100 prediction errors.
questions.erklaerung: `samples` is the number of training instances that reach the node. `value` stores the class counts for those instances.

questions.question: 8. A leaf contains 40 samples: 4 from class A, 30 from class B, and 6 from class C. What probability would the tree estimate for class B?
questions.explanation: The estimated class probability is the proportion of samples of that class in the leaf. For class B, this is 30 divided by 40, which is 0.75.

mini_glossary.Class probability: The estimated chance that an instance belongs to a class.
mini_glossary.Leaf proportion: The fraction of leaf samples belonging to a class.
mini_glossary.predict_proba: A method that returns estimated class probabilities.

extended_explanation.title: Estimating probability from a leaf
extended_explanation.content: A decision tree does not compute a smooth probability function over the feature space. It returns the same class probabilities for every input that lands in the same leaf.
steps: Identify the leaf reached by the instance.
Count the samples of the target class in that leaf.
Divide this count by the total number of leaf samples.

questions.frage: 8. A leaf contains 40 samples: 4 from class A, 30 from class B, and 6 from class C. What probability would the tree estimate for class B?
questions.erklaerung: The estimated class probability is the proportion of samples of that class in the leaf. For class B, this is 30 divided by 40, which is 0.75.

questions.question: 9. In the CART algorithm for classification, how is a split selected at a node?
options: By choosing the feature with the largest numeric range
By selecting the threshold that gives the deepest tree
By choosing a random valid split at each node
By minimizing weighted impurity in the child nodes
questions.explanation: CART evaluates candidate feature-threshold splits. It selects the split with the lowest weighted impurity in the child nodes.

mini_glossary.Threshold: A numerical cutoff used to split a feature.
mini_glossary.Weighted impurity: Impurity of child nodes adjusted by their sample counts.

extended_explanation.title: Choosing a CART split
extended_explanation.content: CART makes a locally optimal choice at each node. This usually works well, but it does not guarantee the globally best tree.
steps: Consider possible features.
Consider candidate thresholds for those features.
Compute the weighted impurity of the resulting child nodes.
Choose the split with the lowest cost.

questions.frage: 9. In the CART algorithm for classification, how is a split selected at a node?
optionen: By choosing the feature with the largest numeric range
By selecting the threshold that gives the deepest tree
By choosing a random valid split at each node
By minimizing weighted impurity in the child nodes
questions.erklaerung: CART evaluates candidate feature-threshold splits. It selects the split with the lowest weighted impurity in the child nodes.

questions.question: 10. Why is CART described as a greedy algorithm?
options: It stores every local split before training begins.
It chooses the best current split without optimizing the full tree.
It tries to include every feature in every path.
It grows a balanced tree before checking impurity.
questions.explanation: CART is greedy because it optimizes the current split locally. It does not search over all possible complete tree structures.

mini_glossary.Greedy algorithm: An algorithm that makes the best local choice at each step.
mini_glossary.Local optimum: The best choice within the current step.
mini_glossary.Global optimum: The best solution across the whole problem.

extended_explanation.title: Greedy tree growth
extended_explanation.content: The greedy strategy keeps training computationally feasible. The trade-off is that the resulting tree may be good without being globally optimal.
steps: CART evaluates splits at the current node.
It selects the split with the best immediate impurity reduction.
It repeats this procedure for child nodes.
It does not test all possible complete tree structures.

questions.frage: 10. Why is CART described as a greedy algorithm?
optionen: It stores every local split before training begins.
It chooses the best current split without optimizing the full tree.
It tries to include every feature in every path.
It grows a balanced tree before checking impurity.
questions.erklaerung: CART is greedy because it optimizes the current split locally. It does not search over all possible complete tree structures.

questions.question: 11. Which statement best compares Gini impurity and entropy in decision trees?
options: Entropy is normally used for regression, while Gini is used for classification.
Gini requires scaled features, while entropy works on unscaled features.
They usually give similar trees, with Gini often being slightly faster.
They normally rank every candidate split identically.
questions.explanation: Gini impurity and entropy are both measures of class mixture in a node. They often produce similar trees, while Gini impurity is commonly used as a slightly faster default.

mini_glossary.Entropy: An impurity measure related to information content.
mini_glossary.Gini impurity: An impurity measure based on class proportions.
mini_glossary.criterion: The hyperparameter that selects the impurity measure.

extended_explanation.title: Selecting an impurity measure
extended_explanation.content: The choice between Gini impurity and entropy is rarely the dominant modeling decision. Regularization and data quality often matter more.
steps: Both Gini impurity and entropy measure class mixture.
Both can guide split selection.
Gini impurity is often computationally simpler.
Differences are usually modest in practical trees.

questions.frage: 11. Which statement best compares Gini impurity and entropy in decision trees?
optionen: Entropy is normally used for regression, while Gini is used for classification.
Gini requires scaled features, while entropy works on unscaled features.
They usually give similar trees, with Gini often being slightly faster.
They normally rank every candidate split identically.
questions.erklaerung: Gini impurity and entropy are both measures of class mixture in a node. They often produce similar trees, while Gini impurity is commonly used as a slightly faster default.

questions.question: 12. A decision tree fits the training data almost perfectly but performs poorly on test data. Which change is most likely to help?
options: Reduce `max_depth` or increase a `min_*` regularization parameter.
Increase `max_depth` to capture more training details.
Remove target labels from the training dataset.
Disable probability estimates before evaluating the tree.
questions.explanation: High training performance with poor test performance is a typical sign of overfitting. Restricting the tree's flexibility can improve generalization.

mini_glossary.max_depth: A limit on how deep the tree may grow.
mini_glossary.min_samples_leaf: The minimum number of samples allowed in a leaf.

extended_explanation.title: Reducing overfitting
extended_explanation.content: Decision trees can fit complex training patterns very closely. Regularization restricts that flexibility so that the model is less likely to memorize noise.
steps: Identify the pattern: high training score and lower test score.
Interpret this pattern as excessive model flexibility.
Limit tree growth using `max_depth` or related parameters.
Validate the change on held-out data.

questions.frage: 12. A decision tree fits the training data almost perfectly but performs poorly on test data. Which change is most likely to help?
optionen: Reduce `max_depth` or increase a `min_*` regularization parameter.
Increase `max_depth` to capture more training details.
Remove target labels from the training dataset.
Disable probability estimates before evaluating the tree.
questions.erklaerung: High training performance with poor test performance is a typical sign of overfitting. Restricting the tree's flexibility can improve generalization.

questions.question: 13. What is the direct effect of increasing `min_samples_leaf`?
options: It changes the impurity measure used at each split.
It increases the number of available input features.
It converts class predictions into numerical predictions.
It requires each leaf to contain more samples.
questions.explanation: `min_samples_leaf` sets the minimum number of samples that each leaf must contain. Increasing it usually produces smoother, less fragmented decision regions.

mini_glossary.min_samples_leaf: Minimum number of samples required in a leaf.
mini_glossary.Leaf size: The number of samples contained in a leaf.
mini_glossary.Decision region: A region of feature space assigned to a prediction.

extended_explanation.title: Controlling leaf granularity
extended_explanation.content: The parameter is especially useful when a tree produces many small regions around individual training samples.
steps: A small leaf size allows very specific rules.
Very specific rules can capture noise.
Increasing `min_samples_leaf` prevents tiny leaves.
This often improves test performance on noisy data.

questions.frage: 13. What is the direct effect of increasing `min_samples_leaf`?
optionen: It changes the impurity measure used at each split.
It increases the number of available input features.
It converts class predictions into numerical predictions.
It requires each leaf to contain more samples.
questions.erklaerung: `min_samples_leaf` sets the minimum number of samples that each leaf must contain. Increasing it usually produces smoother, less fragmented decision regions.

questions.question: 14. Two trees are trained on the same noisy dataset. Tree A has no restrictions and scores 0.99 on training data but 0.89 on test data. Tree B uses `min_samples_leaf = 5` and scores 0.94 on training data but 0.92 on test data. Which interpretation is best?
options: Tree A is preferable because its training accuracy is higher.
Tree B is invalid because regularized trees cannot generalize.
Tree B likely generalizes better despite lower training accuracy.
Both trees are equivalent because they use the same algorithm.
Tree A underfits because its test score is lower than its training score.
questions.explanation: The test score is the better indicator of performance on new data. Tree B trades some training accuracy for better test accuracy, which suggests improved generalization.

mini_glossary.Training accuracy: Accuracy on data used to fit the model.
mini_glossary.Regularized tree: A tree whose complexity is constrained.

extended_explanation.title: Comparing train and test scores
extended_explanation.content: A very high training score can be misleading for flexible models. The goal is not to memorize the training set but to perform well on unseen data.
steps: Compare training and test performance for each model.
Identify the gap between them.
Prefer the model with stronger test performance.
Interpret reduced training accuracy as acceptable when test accuracy improves.

questions.frage: 14. Two trees are trained on the same noisy dataset. Tree A has no restrictions and scores 0.99 on training data but 0.89 on test data. Tree B uses `min_samples_leaf = 5` and scores 0.94 on training data but 0.92 on test data. Which interpretation is best?
optionen: Tree A is preferable because its training accuracy is higher.
Tree B is invalid because regularized trees cannot generalize.
Tree B likely generalizes better despite lower training accuracy.
Both trees are equivalent because they use the same algorithm.
Tree A underfits because its test score is lower than its training score.
questions.erklaerung: The test score is the better indicator of performance on new data. Tree B trades some training accuracy for better test accuracy, which suggests improved generalization.

questions.question: 15. In pruning, when is a node commonly considered a candidate for removal?
options: When the node uses a numerical feature threshold.
When its purity gain is not statistically significant.
When it is the root node of the decision tree.
When its child nodes contain different class labels.
questions.explanation: Pruning removes tree structure that is unlikely to provide meaningful improvement. A node can be removed when its apparent purity gain could plausibly be due to chance.

mini_glossary.Pruning: Removing unnecessary branches after or during tree construction.
mini_glossary.p-value: A probability used to assess whether an observed improvement may be due to chance.
mini_glossary.Null hypothesis: The assumption that an observed improvement is random.

extended_explanation.title: Reasoning about pruning
extended_explanation.content: Pruning is another way to regularize trees. Instead of preventing all growth in advance, it can remove unnecessary structure afterward.
steps: Train or grow a candidate tree.
Inspect nodes whose children are leaves.
Estimate whether the purity gain is meaningful.
Remove branches whose gain is not statistically convincing.

questions.frage: 15. In pruning, when is a node commonly considered a candidate for removal?
optionen: When the node uses a numerical feature threshold.
When its purity gain is not statistically significant.
When it is the root node of the decision tree.
When its child nodes contain different class labels.
questions.erklaerung: Pruning removes tree structure that is unlikely to provide meaningful improvement. A node can be removed when its apparent purity gain could plausibly be due to chance.

questions.question: 16. A regression leaf contains target values 2, 4, and 9. What value should the leaf predict under the usual regression-tree rule?
questions.explanation: A regression leaf usually predicts the mean of the target values in that leaf. The mean of 2, 4, and 9 is 5.

mini_glossary.Regression leaf: A leaf that stores a numerical prediction.
mini_glossary.Target values: Observed numerical outputs in the training data.
mini_glossary.Mean prediction: Prediction computed as the average target value.

extended_explanation.title: Computing a regression leaf value
extended_explanation.content: This rule makes the leaf prediction the best constant approximation under squared-error loss for the samples in that leaf.
steps: Collect the target values in the leaf.
Add the values together.
Divide by the number of values.
Use the result as the prediction.

questions.frage: 16. A regression leaf contains target values 2, 4, and 9. What value should the leaf predict under the usual regression-tree rule?
questions.erklaerung: A regression leaf usually predicts the mean of the target values in that leaf. The mean of 2, 4, and 9 is 5.

questions.question: 17. For regression trees, what does CART try to minimize when choosing a split?
options: The weighted mean squared error of the child nodes
The number of class labels in each child node
The alphabetical order of the selected feature names
The depth difference between left and right branches
questions.explanation: In regression, CART tries to reduce numerical prediction error rather than class impurity. It therefore minimizes the weighted mean squared error of the child nodes.

mini_glossary.Mean squared error: The average squared difference between predictions and targets.
mini_glossary.Weighted cost: A cost adjusted by the number of samples in each child node.
mini_glossary.Child node: A node created by splitting a parent node.

extended_explanation.title: Regression split objective
extended_explanation.content: The classification version focuses on class purity. The regression version focuses on making target values inside each child node as close as possible to the child's predicted mean.
steps: Try candidate feature-threshold splits.
Compute the mean squared error in each child node.
Weight each child error by its sample count.
Choose the split with the lowest weighted error.

questions.frage: 17. For regression trees, what does CART try to minimize when choosing a split?
optionen: The weighted mean squared error of the child nodes
The number of class labels in each child node
The alphabetical order of the selected feature names
The depth difference between left and right branches
questions.erklaerung: In regression, CART tries to reduce numerical prediction error rather than class impurity. It therefore minimizes the weighted mean squared error of the child nodes.

questions.question: 18. Why are predictions with a trained, reasonably balanced decision tree usually fast?
options: The model removes features before prediction.
The tree retrains itself for each new input.
The tree scans all training samples at each leaf.
Prediction follows a single root-to-leaf path.
questions.explanation: Prediction follows one route from the root to a leaf. In a reasonably balanced tree, that route grows roughly logarithmically with the number of training samples.

mini_glossary.Balanced tree: A tree whose branches have roughly similar depths.
mini_glossary.Path length: The number of nodes visited during prediction.
mini_glossary.Logarithmic growth: Growth that increases slowly as input size grows.

extended_explanation.title: Prediction complexity
extended_explanation.content: The tree does not scan the full training set during prediction. It only evaluates the sequence of tests along the selected path.
steps: Start at the root.
Evaluate one test per visited node.
Follow only one branch at each split.
Stop when a leaf is reached.

questions.frage: 18. Why are predictions with a trained, reasonably balanced decision tree usually fast?
optionen: The model removes features before prediction.
The tree retrains itself for each new input.
The tree scans all training samples at each leaf.
Prediction follows a single root-to-leaf path.
questions.erklaerung: Prediction follows one route from the root to a leaf. In a reasonably balanced tree, that route grows roughly logarithmically with the number of training samples.

questions.question: 19. If the number of features doubles while the number of samples stays the same, what usually happens to CART training time?
options: It decreases because each split becomes simpler.
It increases roughly in proportion to the number of features.
It remains unchanged because prediction complexity is logarithmic.
It becomes independent of the number of samples.
questions.explanation: CART compares candidate splits across available features. If the number of features doubles, the split search usually becomes roughly twice as expensive.

mini_glossary.Training time: The time required to fit the model.
mini_glossary.Feature: An input variable used by the model.
mini_glossary.Split candidate: A possible feature-threshold choice considered during training.

extended_explanation.title: Why more features cost more
extended_explanation.content: Prediction may still be fast after training, but the search for good splits becomes more expensive as the feature space grows.
steps: CART must evaluate candidate splits.
Candidate splits depend on features and thresholds.
Doubling features increases the number of feature choices.
Training time therefore tends to rise substantially.

questions.frage: 19. If the number of features doubles while the number of samples stays the same, what usually happens to CART training time?
optionen: It decreases because each split becomes simpler.
It increases roughly in proportion to the number of features.
It remains unchanged because prediction complexity is logarithmic.
It becomes independent of the number of samples.
questions.erklaerung: CART compares candidate splits across available features. If the number of features doubles, the split search usually becomes roughly twice as expensive.

questions.question: 20. Why can rotating a dataset make a decision tree's boundary unnecessarily complex?
options: They use axis-aligned splits based on one feature at a time.
They discard numerical features after a rotation.
They replace thresholds with class probabilities.
They convert classification boundaries into regression targets.
questions.explanation: Standard decision trees split on one feature at a time. This creates axis-aligned boundaries, so diagonal separations may require staircase-like structures.

mini_glossary.Axis-aligned split: A split based on one feature at a time.
mini_glossary.Rotation: A transformation that changes the orientation of the data.

extended_explanation.title: Axis alignment effect
extended_explanation.content: This limitation does not make decision trees useless. It means their performance can depend strongly on how the feature space is represented.
steps: A tree split tests one feature against a threshold.
Such splits create boundaries parallel to feature axes.
A diagonal pattern cannot be captured directly by one split.
The tree may build a staircase-like approximation.

questions.frage: 20. Why can rotating a dataset make a decision tree's boundary unnecessarily complex?
optionen: They use axis-aligned splits based on one feature at a time.
They discard numerical features after a rotation.
They replace thresholds with class probabilities.
They convert classification boundaries into regression targets.
questions.erklaerung: Standard decision trees split on one feature at a time. This creates axis-aligned boundaries, so diagonal separations may require staircase-like structures.

questions.question: 21. Why might a pipeline with scaling followed by PCA help before training a decision tree on some datasets?
options: It removes the need to choose split thresholds.
It changes the labels into principal components.
It can align useful separations with tree-friendly axes.
It guarantees a shallower tree for every dataset.
questions.explanation: PCA can rotate the feature space into new components. In some datasets, this makes useful separations easier to capture with axis-aligned tree splits.

mini_glossary.PCA: A method that creates new orthogonal components from existing features.
mini_glossary.Scaling: Transforming features to comparable numerical ranges.
mini_glossary.Feature space: The coordinate system defined by input features.

extended_explanation.title: Preprocessing for tree geometry
extended_explanation.content: Decision trees do not usually require scaling for basic training. Here scaling is useful because PCA itself is sensitive to feature scales.
steps: Scale features so that PCA behaves appropriately.
Use PCA to rotate the coordinate system.
Train the tree on the transformed features.
Check whether simpler decision boundaries result.

questions.frage: 21. Why might a pipeline with scaling followed by PCA help before training a decision tree on some datasets?
optionen: It removes the need to choose split thresholds.
It changes the labels into principal components.
It can align useful separations with tree-friendly axes.
It guarantees a shallower tree for every dataset.
questions.erklaerung: PCA can rotate the feature space into new components. In some datasets, this makes useful separations easier to capture with axis-aligned tree splits.

questions.question: 22. Why can two decision trees trained on the same data sometimes differ when `random_state` is not fixed?
options: The target variable is overwritten after the first training run.
The training procedure may include random choices.
The impurity measure becomes undefined after fitting.
A tree cannot be fitted twice on the same dataset.
questions.explanation: Some implementations include randomness during training, such as random feature ordering or selection. Setting `random_state` makes that randomness reproducible.

mini_glossary.random_state: A seed parameter used to make random behavior reproducible.
mini_glossary.Variance: Sensitivity of a model to changes in data or training procedure.
mini_glossary.Reproducibility: The ability to obtain the same result again.

extended_explanation.title: Reproducible tree training
extended_explanation.content: Decision trees can have high variance. This means small changes in data or training details may produce noticeably different models.
steps: Training may involve random choices.
Small differences can change early splits.
Early split changes can alter large parts of the tree.
Setting `random_state` fixes the random sequence.

questions.frage: 22. Why can two decision trees trained on the same data sometimes differ when `random_state` is not fixed?
optionen: The target variable is overwritten after the first training run.
The training procedure may include random choices.
The impurity measure becomes undefined after fitting.
A tree cannot be fitted twice on the same dataset.
questions.erklaerung: Some implementations include randomness during training, such as random feature ordering or selection. Setting `random_state` makes that randomness reproducible.

questions.question: 23. Why do Random Forests usually reduce the variance problem of individual decision trees?
options: They replace the trees with a single linear separator.
They remove the variance of every feature before training.
They train one tree and prune it repeatedly.
They aggregate predictions from many different trees.
questions.explanation: A Random Forest combines predictions from many trees. Voting or averaging reduces the influence of any one unstable tree.

mini_glossary.Ensemble: A model built by combining several models.
mini_glossary.Majority vote: A classification rule that chooses the most frequent prediction.

extended_explanation.title: Why ensembles stabilize trees
extended_explanation.content: The strength of a forest comes from diversity among trees and aggregation across them. This often improves generalization compared with a single tree.
steps: Train multiple diverse trees.
Let each tree make a prediction.
Combine their predictions by voting or averaging.
Reduce the influence of individual tree errors.

questions.frage: 23. Why do Random Forests usually reduce the variance problem of individual decision trees?
optionen: They replace the trees with a single linear separator.
They remove the variance of every feature before training.
They train one tree and prune it repeatedly.
They aggregate predictions from many different trees.
questions.erklaerung: A Random Forest combines predictions from many trees. Voting or averaging reduces the influence of any one unstable tree.

questions.question: 24. What does `max_features` control in a decision tree?
options: The maximum number of features considered at a split
The maximum number of class labels in the dataset
The maximum number of probabilities returned by prediction
The maximum number of evaluation runs during testing
questions.explanation: `max_features` limits how many input features are considered when searching for a split. This can affect both training cost and tree structure.

mini_glossary.max_features: The maximum number of features considered for a split.
mini_glossary.Split search: The process of evaluating possible node splits.
mini_glossary.Hyperparameter: A setting chosen before or during model training.

extended_explanation.title: Limiting features per split
extended_explanation.content: Limiting available features can regularize the model and reduce computation. It is also important in tree ensembles.
steps: At each node, the algorithm searches for a good split.
Without limits, many or all features may be considered.
`max_features` restricts this search.
The restriction can change the fitted tree.

questions.frage: 24. What does `max_features` control in a decision tree?
optionen: The maximum number of features considered at a split
The maximum number of class labels in the dataset
The maximum number of probabilities returned by prediction
The maximum number of evaluation runs during testing
questions.erklaerung: `max_features` limits how many input features are considered when searching for a split. This can affect both training cost and tree structure.

questions.question: 25. Which setting most directly prevents a node with too few samples from being split further?
options: `criterion = "entropy"`
`random_state = 42`
`min_samples_split`
`max_features = 1`
questions.explanation: `min_samples_split` defines how many samples a node must contain before it may be split. If the node has fewer samples, it remains a leaf.

mini_glossary.min_samples_split: Minimum number of samples required to split a node.
mini_glossary.Internal node: A node that performs a split.
mini_glossary.Stopping condition: A rule that prevents further tree growth.

extended_explanation.title: Stopping tree growth
extended_explanation.content: This parameter reduces overly specific branches. It is useful when the tree would otherwise split small groups of noisy samples.
steps: Check how many samples reach a node.
Compare this number with `min_samples_split`.
Allow splitting only if the minimum is met.
Otherwise keep the node as a leaf.

questions.frage: 25. Which setting most directly prevents a node with too few samples from being split further?
optionen: `criterion = "entropy"`
`random_state = 42`
`min_samples_split`
`max_features = 1`
questions.erklaerung: `min_samples_split` defines how many samples a node must contain before it may be split. If the node has fewer samples, it remains a leaf.

questions.question: 26. A dataset has a diagonal class boundary. Model A is a decision tree trained on the original features and creates many staircase-like regions. Model B uses scaled PCA features before the tree and reaches similar accuracy with fewer splits. Which conclusion is most justified?
options: PCA removed the need for thresholds in the tree.
The transformed feature space better matches axis-aligned splits.
The preprocessing step changed the target labels.
The original tree is invalid because it used diagonal data.
questions.explanation: A standard tree creates axis-aligned splits. PCA can rotate the feature space, making some diagonal structures easier to represent compactly.

mini_glossary.Diagonal boundary: A separation not aligned with the original feature axes.
mini_glossary.PCA features: New features formed as linear combinations of original features.
mini_glossary.Compact tree: A tree that represents a pattern with relatively few splits.

extended_explanation.title: Analyzing feature-space geometry
extended_explanation.content: The central issue is not that PCA makes the tree more powerful in every case. It changes the coordinate system, which can make some patterns easier for axis-aligned splits to express.
steps: Identify the geometry of the class boundary.
Recall that tree splits are axis-aligned.
Recognize that PCA can rotate feature axes.
Explain why fewer splits may then be sufficient.

questions.frage: 26. A dataset has a diagonal class boundary. Model A is a decision tree trained on the original features and creates many staircase-like regions. Model B uses scaled PCA features before the tree and reaches similar accuracy with fewer splits. Which conclusion is most justified?
optionen: PCA removed the need for thresholds in the tree.
The transformed feature space better matches axis-aligned splits.
The preprocessing step changed the target labels.
The original tree is invalid because it used diagonal data.
questions.erklaerung: A standard tree creates axis-aligned splits. PCA can rotate the feature space, making some diagonal structures easier to represent compactly.

questions.question: 27. A node contains 20 samples: 10 from class A and 10 from class B. What is its Gini impurity?
questions.explanation: The class proportions are 0.5 and 0.5. The Gini impurity is 1 minus the sum of squared class proportions, so it is 1 minus 0.25 minus 0.25, which equals 0.50.

mini_glossary.Class proportion: The fraction of samples in a node belonging to a class.
mini_glossary.Squared proportion: A class proportion multiplied by itself.
mini_glossary.Gini impurity: One minus the sum of squared class proportions.

extended_explanation.title: Gini calculation
extended_explanation.content: This node is mixed evenly between two classes. Therefore it is not pure, but it is also not maximally mixed across many classes.
steps: Compute the class proportions: 10 divided by 20 and 10 divided by 20.
Square each proportion.
Add the squared proportions.
Subtract the result from 1.

questions.frage: 27. A node contains 20 samples: 10 from class A and 10 from class B. What is its Gini impurity?
questions.erklaerung: The class proportions are 0.5 and 0.5. The Gini impurity is 1 minus the sum of squared class proportions, so it is 1 minus 0.25 minus 0.25, which equals 0.50.

questions.question: 28. A tree has a very deep structure, many leaves with only one or two samples, and unstable predictions near isolated training points. Which diagnosis and remedy fit best?
options: The tree is likely overfitting; regularization should constrain its structure.
The tree is likely underfitting; all constraints should be removed.
The tree is invalid because nonlinear patterns cannot be modeled.
The tree is too interpretable; the target variable should be replaced.
questions.explanation: Tiny leaves and unstable local predictions are typical signs of overfitting. Regularization can force the tree to use broader, more reliable regions.

mini_glossary.Tiny leaf: A leaf containing very few samples.
mini_glossary.Model complexity: The flexibility of a model's fitted structure.
mini_glossary.Stable prediction: A prediction that does not change sharply due to small input changes.

extended_explanation.title: Diagnosing overfitting structurally
extended_explanation.content: The problem is structural rather than merely numerical. A tree with many tiny leaves has enough flexibility to encode accidental patterns in the training set.
steps: Observe the depth and number of leaves.
Check whether leaves contain very few samples.
Link small leaves to memorization of training noise.
Select regularization that limits tree flexibility.

questions.frage: 28. A tree has a very deep structure, many leaves with only one or two samples, and unstable predictions near isolated training points. Which diagnosis and remedy fit best?
optionen: The tree is likely overfitting; regularization should constrain its structure.
The tree is likely underfitting; all constraints should be removed.
The tree is invalid because nonlinear patterns cannot be modeled.
The tree is too interpretable; the target variable should be replaced.
questions.erklaerung: Tiny leaves and unstable local predictions are typical signs of overfitting. Regularization can force the tree to use broader, more reliable regions.

questions.question: 29. Suppose training a decision tree on 1,000,000 samples takes 1 hour. If the number of samples increases to 10,000,000 and the number of features stays fixed, which estimate best matches CART's approximate training complexity?
options: About 2 hours
About 5 hours
About 10 hours
About 12 hours
questions.explanation: CART training scales roughly with the number of samples times a logarithmic factor in the number of samples. Increasing from 1,000,000 to 10,000,000 samples gives a little more than a tenfold increase, approximately 11 to 12 hours.

mini_glossary.Asymptotic complexity: A description of how cost grows with input size.
mini_glossary.Logarithmic factor: A slowly growing multiplier involving the logarithm of sample size.

extended_explanation.title: Estimating training-time growth
extended_explanation.content: The logarithmic term grows slowly, but it is not zero. That is why the estimate is somewhat larger than exactly ten hours.
steps: Use the approximate dependence on sample count and its logarithm.
Multiply by 10 for the tenfold increase in samples.
Account for the larger logarithmic factor.
Choose an estimate slightly above 10 hours.

questions.frage: 29. Suppose training a decision tree on 1,000,000 samples takes 1 hour. If the number of samples increases to 10,000,000 and the number of features stays fixed, which estimate best matches CART's approximate training complexity?
optionen: About 2 hours
About 5 hours
About 10 hours
About 12 hours
questions.erklaerung: CART training scales roughly with the number of samples times a logarithmic factor in the number of samples. Increasing from 1,000,000 to 10,000,000 samples gives a little more than a tenfold increase, approximately 11 to 12 hours.

questions.question: 30. You train 1,000 small decision trees on different random subsets of the same training data. Each tree is weaker than a full tree, but majority voting improves test accuracy. What is the best explanation?
options: The individual trees became deterministic linear classifiers.
Voting reduced the effect of individual high-variance errors.
The weak trees no longer used the target variable.
The test set was used as training data.
questions.explanation: Individual trees can vary strongly, especially when trained on different samples. Majority voting can cancel out many individual errors and produce a more stable prediction.

mini_glossary.High variance: Strong sensitivity to changes in training data.
mini_glossary.Majority voting: Combining classifiers by selecting the most frequent predicted class.
mini_glossary.Subset sampling: Training models on different random parts of the data.

extended_explanation.title: Analyzing the ensemble effect
extended_explanation.content: The ensemble does not require every tree to be highly accurate. It relies on many diverse trees making errors that do not all point in the same direction.
steps: Recognize that each small tree may be individually noisy.
Use different subsets to create diverse errors.
Aggregate predictions by majority vote.
Interpret the improvement as variance reduction.

questions.frage: 30. You train 1,000 small decision trees on different random subsets of the same training data. Each tree is weaker than a full tree, but majority voting improves test accuracy. What is the best explanation?
optionen: The individual trees became deterministic linear classifiers.
Voting reduced the effect of individual high-variance errors.
The weak trees no longer used the target variable.
The test set was used as training data.
questions.erklaerung: Individual trees can vary strongly, especially when trained on different samples. Majority voting can cancel out many individual errors and produce a more stable prediction.
